entry.title: Feuerwerk - Klone deine Rekursion: Einheit 2 - Lektion 5
entry.content:
An English version of this lesson is available at: Get Coding with Snap! - Building Up to AI

 

Videolektion "Feuerwerk"

Diese Lektion kombiniert die Konzepte der letzten beiden Lektionen - Rekursion und Klone - im wahrsten Sinne zu einem Feuerwerk. 

Folge den Schritten aus dem Video in Snap!. Die Aufgaben bauen teilweise auf das Projekt aus dem Video auf. 

 

 

 

 

Du bist dran

  • Importiere das Modul “Animation” in einem neuen Projekt und experimentiere mit den Blöcken. Was kannst du mit ihnen machen?  
    • Lasse ein Objekt realistisch springen, indem du es bescheunigst und abbremst.
    • Animiere ein Objekt so, dass es fällt und realistisch vom Boden abprallt (du kannst dafür sogar einen rekursiven Block erstellen).

 

  • Versuche die “Herz-Explosion” aus dem Video nachzubauen. Mache sie interssanter:
    • setze Grafikeffekte ein
    • wähle die Größe der Klone zufällig
    • erstelle verschiedene Kostüme
    • füge einen Hintergrund hinzu
    • werde kreativ!

  • Hier findest du die Aufgaben auch zum Herunterladen:

    • als PDF: https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/6ihs5rZ?rc=10&doi=SAP1327937 
    • als editierbare docx Version:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/GGT4sfX?rc=10&doi=SAP1327976 

    Lösungsvorschläge für Einheit 2 - Lektion 4 findest du in dieser Collection.

 

 

Zusammenfassung - das hast du diese Lektion gelernt

Bausteine dieser Lektion: 

Lässt das Objekt, das im ersten Eingabefeld ausgewählt ist, die Blöcke im zweiten Eingabefeld - dem Ring - ausführen. 
Kann auch in Verbindung mit dem neuer Klon Block verwendet werden, um mit nur einem Block eine Klon zu erzeugen und seine Reaktion zu triggern.

Berichtet einen neuen Klon des Objekts, das den Block ausführt. Kann zum Beispiel als Eingabe für den lasse Block verwendet werden, um einen Klon zu erzeugen und seine Reaktion im selben Skript direkt zu triggern. 

Startet einen neuen Prozess (Thread), der die Blöcke im Ring ausführt.
Die Blöcke im Ring laufen parallel zu den Blöcken, die nach dem starte Block kommen ab. 

 

Parallelismus II

In dieser Lektion hast du eine weitere Möglichkeit kennengelernt, Skripte parallel auszuführen, nämlich mit dem starte Block.

Der starte Block besitzt ein Eingabefeld mit einem grauen Ring, das Befehlsblöcke erwartet. Die Befehlsblöcke im grauen Ring werden in einem separaten Prozess (Thread) ausgeführt. Das bedeutet, dass sie parallel zu den Blöcken ablaufen, die auf den starte Block folgen.

Die beiden folgenden Skripte spielen jeweils drei Töne. Das erste Skript spielt sie nacheinander – als Arpeggio –, sodass jeder Ton erst beginnt, nachdem der vorherige beendet ist. Im zweiten Skript starten alle drei Töne gleichzeitig, dadurch entsteht ein Akkord mit der Dauer eines Schlags.

Führt man die drei "spiele Note" Blöcke wie oben aus, führt das zu einem Arpeggio - die Töne werden nacheinander gespielt. Verwendet man die starte-Blöcke wird für jede Note ein neuer Prozess gestartet und ein Akkord entsteht, bei dem die drei Noten parallel gespielt werden.

 

Im Video haben wir den starte Block verwendet, damit alle Herz-Klone gleichzeitig explodieren und ihre Skripte parallel ausführen.

Wenn du den Block starte weglässt, führt jeder einzelne Herz-Klon zunächst sein gesamtes Skript aus. Erst nachdem dieses vollständig beendet ist, wird der nächste Klon erzeugt. 

Wenn die Klone mit dem "starte Block" explodieren, werden die Explosionen parallel und nicht der Reihe nach ausgeführt.

 

 

Und jetzt?

Das wars mit Einheit 2 - Lektion 5: Feuerwerk - Klone deine Rekursion.

  • Zur nächsten Lektion des Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/zum-guten-schluss-der-knaller-einheit-2-video-6/ba-p/14447067 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite von Einheit 2 geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/einheit-2-bausteine-f%C3%BCr-syst... 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite des gesamten Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/programmieren-mit-snap-building-u... 
  • Eine Englische Version des Kurses, bei der ein Zertifikat erworben werden kann, findet sich hier: https://learning.sap.com/courses/get-coding-with-snap-building-up-to-ai 

 

Dieses Material ist veröffentlicht unter der CC 4.0 Lizenz durch das Snap! Team bei SAP

entry.title: What's new in asset management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608?
entry.content:
Hello and welcome back to my blog post! Happy to have you here!

With every release, SAP works closely with customers and partners to address real pain points that maintenance professionals deal every day. The 2608 release is no different and I'm excited to walk you through what we've shipped this time.

Below, you’ll find a curated selection of release highlights from our core product management team. Before diving in, I invite you to watch the release highlights video, featuring my AI avatar 🤖

 

But before we dive into the release highlights, I want to set up some context. SAP is investing heavily in AI for asset management. If you've been following SAP's direction toward the autonomous enterprise, you know the strategic focus on fundamentally changing how maintenance supervisors triage thousands of notifications from IoT-connected equipment, how planners navigate fragmented data across multiple apps to make planning decisions, and how technicians report defects in the field.

Today, these personas spend too much time collecting information from different sources. The vision is clear: AI agents, Joule-based, conversational, and accessible in natural language, that consolidate this intelligence and deliver it on demand, so your teams can stop reacting to failures and start predicting them. I'll share more on this at the end of this post, including agents that are in preview and on the roadmap for later releases.

If you've been following my previous blog posts (2602, 2508), you know we've been steadily building on the maintenance planner experience, permit-to-work capabilities, and overall app usability. This release continues that trajectory with features that I believe will make a tangible difference in your day-to-day work.

Here's what we're covering in this post:

  • Asset overview app enhancements
  • Permit to work enhancements
  • Dispatch maintenance orders from maintenance order apps
  • Manage maintenance notifications improvements
  • Looking ahead: business AI for asset management

We've also recorded short demo videos for some innovations so you can see them working in the system. You'll find them embedded throughout this post.

Asset overview app enhancements

Some of you might already be aware that in 2602, we released the Asset Overview app. The main purpose of this Fiori app is to have a complete 360-degree view of a technical object in a single screen, unifying static data and a complete holistic view of all information around that technical object. It helps all maintenance personnel get a complete picture of what is happening on their equipment or functional location.

Planners, supervisors, and master-data specialists can now get a quick glimpse into running maintenance plans, find attached files, view bill of materials, and create emergency work, all without leaving the Asset Overview. Here's what's new:

  • A thumbnail view: you can click the image and see a picture of the technical object, which helps in clearly identifying what the equipment is about
  • An attachments tab that provides the list of all documents associated with the technical object. You can view and open directly from this screen
  • Quick actions to create emergency work or minor work notifications directly from the Asset Overview. Creation of notifications was already available in the last release, but now we've added the ability to create minor work and emergency work specifically. Emergency work automatically creates the work order in the background, so the new order immediately appears in your open order list
  • A new maintenance plan tab that displays all maintenance plans and items associated with the specific technical object. If there are multiple plans, they are all listed with important information. You can click into a maintenance plan and navigate directly to the Manage Maintenance Plans Fiori app. No need to search for it separately
  • A new bill of materials tab that pulls the BOM master associated with the technical object and lists all components that are part of it, without going to a separate BOM application

The hierarchy view (expanding superior functional locations, sub-functional locations, and associated equipment) was already available in the last release. These new tabs and actions build on that foundation.

The bottom line: you get a holistic view of all proactive maintenance set up for your equipment or functional location without opening another app. For those of you managing large asset portfolios, this consolidation should save meaningful time every day.

 

 

Permit to work enhancements 

Permit to work is a process that was implemented in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition in the last release. Its purpose is to ensure that all maintenance work is executed in a safe environment, and that all precautionary measures are taken so that work is done in the safest manner. In 2608, there are several enhancements to this process.

Flexible approval workflow 

Until now, the permit approval process was fairly rigid: permits could move from creation to issuance without a formal approval gate. Many of you have told us that your organizations need different approval flows depending on the type of work, the risk level, or the team involved. We listened.  With this release, you can now define flexible, rule-based approval workflows for permits.

The configuration is self-service for administrators and works on a plant + permit type combination model. What does this mean in practice: 

  • You can define your own permit approval process. For example, a hot work permit in one plant may require no approval, while a cold work permit in the same plant requires full approval. You control this per combination 
  • Configure approvals to be acted upon by an individual user, a specific role, or a team of approvers 
  • Choose between "1 approver suffices" or "All must approve" formats depending on your business needs 
  • Add conditions to control when specific steps are triggered 
  • Initiation can be parameter-driven (for example, by permit type or planning context), so different permits follow different approval paths 
  • Full rework cycle supported: if an approver rejects, they can provide comments (e.g., "dates need to change"), the planner sees the rejection reason, makes edits, and resubmits for approval — all tracked in the system 
  • Full logging and audit trail for every step, so you always know who approved what and when 
  • A new document type (YPW work permit) is available in the Manage Permit application, giving you a dedicated attachment category for permit-specific documents with a unique identifier for better document management 
  • Permits can be handed out to technicians or contingent workers, with the appropriate value help for each 

For organizations in regulated industries or with strict safety protocols, this is a significant step forward. You get clear accountability, no more workarounds with email approvals, and a proper audit trail built into the process. Without approval being in place, the permit simply cannot be issued or handed out — the system enforces the control. 

 

View and report on permits assigned to order 

The second permit-related enhancement is about visibility. A common scenario: you are a maintenance planner assigning a job to a technician, and you need to verify whether the right permits are in place. Previously, you had to leave the maintenance order and check in a separate app. 

We've  now added: 

  • A new "Permits" tab directly in Change Maintenance Order and Display Maintenance Order (WDA app), showing all permit-related information and permits assigned to that order 
  • The same permit tab is available in Manage Maintenance Notification and Order and in Find Maintenance Order — the new tab appears on the object page 
  • A new column in Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders that lets you filter and report on jobs with work permits quickly 

This means planners and supervisors can verify permits are in place before assigning work, right within the context they're already working in. No cross-referencing, no switching apps. It supports safer, more audit-ready maintenance execution. 

Dispatch maintenance orders from maintenance order apps

Previously, you could not dispatch a work order from the Change Maintenance Order application or the Find Maintenance Order application if the work order's work center was maintained in the My Work Center list in the Resource Scheduling (RSH) application. That was a limitation: if a work center was in that list, dispatch could only happen through the RSH-relevant application. It could not be dispatched from the normal maintenance order apps.

We've removed that restriction. In 2608, you can dispatch the work order from the Change Maintenance Order or Find Maintenance Order application, regardless of whether the work center is maintained in the My Work Center list in the RSH application or not.

  • In Change Maintenance Order: go to Additional Functions → Dispatch Operation. All operations get a dispatch status, and the phase moves to execution
  • In Find Maintenance Order: select the order(s), press the Dispatch Operation button to dispatch
  • Cancel dispatch is also available: if all operations are in dispatch status, the Cancel Dispatch Operation button becomes enabled, resetting status back to released and scheduling back to scheduled

Important: dispatch currently happens at the header level. All operations associated with the work order are dispatched together. There is no capability yet to select individual operations for dispatch. That functionality is on the roadmap.

Manage maintenance notifications improvements

If you're a supervisor screening dozens of notifications daily, you know how small friction points add up. The Manage Maintenance Notifications Fiori app (F5777) continues to get incremental improvements with each release — and in 2608, three of them target exactly that: reducing repetitive clicks, preventing duplicates, and removing manual steps that shouldn't be manual.

Open notifications alert: When you create a notification, the system now checks whether open notifications already exist for that technical object. If they do, an info message strip appears with a direct hyperlink to view them.

Reset deletion flag from the list: You can now select one or more notifications of similar status and reset the deletion flag directly in the app, as a mass change capability.

Order type defaulting: When creating an order from a maintenance request, the order type now defaults automatically based on the configuration that maps notification types to order types. The user no longer needs to drill down and select from the list — it defaults automatically. Of course, the user can still change it if needed.

 

Roadmap: AI agents for asset management

SAP is heavily investing on the AI side. In the coming few months, we will be delivering a set of AI agents. These are all Joule-based agents: you open Joule, converse in natural language, and the agent responds with the data you're looking for. Four agents are currently on development:

Functional Location Insights Agent: The Functional Location Insights Agent consolidates master data, warranty status, cost center assignments, failure trends, recurring spare parts, maintenance cost trends, proactive maintenance schedules, and full maintenance history into a single on-demand summary, eliminating the need to navigate multiple SAP transactions.

Maintenance Activity Insights Agent: The Maintenance Activity Insights Agent provides a unified, end-to-end view of maintenance orders by consolidating all relevant data: costs, materials, operations, confirmations, and notes, into a single interface. It empowers maintenance planners and supervisors to quickly validate execution efficiency, identify deviations, and ensure accurate closure without navigating multiple transactions.

Maintenance Plan Cost Simulation Agent: The Maintenance Plan Cost Simulation Agent consolidates data from various sources to provide a detailed view of preventive maintenance costs, including planned, actual, and projected costs. It enables maintenance planners to analyze cost patterns and anomalies quickly and effectively, ultimately facilitating faster, cost-effective maintenance planning and execution.

Maintenance Request Screening Agent: The Maintenance Request Screening Agent helps maintenance supervisors rank notifications by priority, detect duplicates, and provide AI-generated summaries and recommendations. It reduces manual triage effort, improves prioritization quality, and lowers maintenance costs by ensuring the most critical work is prioritized first, ultimately enhancing operational efficiency.

Note: These agents are planned for delivery in the next releases. They are not part of the 2608 general availability scope. Follow the roadmap for more updates

This is the direction we are heading: from reactive firefighting to proactive command. The human remains in the loop, but AI supports with intelligence so your experts can focus on decisions, not data collection.

If you want to see one of the AI agents in action and hear more about the AI vision from our product management team, check out our recent SAP Expert Talk where I sat down with Akif Ünal and Amar Kumar to discuss what's coming:

Learn more and stay connected

That's it for the 2608 asset management highlights. If you want to explore further:

  • SAP Road Map Explorer – search for asset management to see the full roadmap
  • SAP Help Portal – documentation for the latest release
  • SAP Community for Asset Management – ask questions, share experiences, and connect with peers
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition page and release information
  • Asset Management learning journey – follow the guided path and get certified

If you're already on Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, now is also a good time to explore what you have not activated yet — there are capabilities available that you might not be using. And if any of these 2608 features are relevant to your organization, or if there's something you'd like to see in future releases, drop a comment below.

 Feel free to follow me at in the community and on LinkedIn to not miss any updates. 

Find all our assets  

The release 2608 showcases more than 130 innovation highlights across the portfolio. Check out our release document for all the details!

entry.title: From clicks to intent: Why SAP SuccessFactors gets stronger in the agent era
entry.content:
There's a phrase making the rounds in customer meetings and blog posts that's worth taking seriously: "death by AI." The argument goes that as AI agents learn to do the work, the software underneath them becomes a commodity — the SaaS suites we've used for years and keep expanding quietly lose their reason to exist.

So it's a fair question to put on the table: does that apply to SAP SuccessFactors?

I've been living with this question for a while, and my answer is a confident no. But I don't want to wave the concern away, because it points at something real — and the response it demands from us is more urgent than "relax, we're fine."

The good, the bad, and the ugly

The good news first: AI doesn't kill SaaS as a category. What it does kill is undifferentiated, UI-centric software — tools that live or die by the screens you navigate and lack three things: depth of data, authority over the process, and gravity within a larger platform. SAP SuccessFactors has all three, so it isn't threatened at the level of existence.

The bad news is that the pressure is still real, and it lands somewhere specific. It's not about functionality. It's about how people interact with the system — and that's the part that's changing fastest.

The ugly part? The work to be ready for it exposes every shortcut we've taken with our data and processes over the years. More on that later.

From clicks to intent

Think about how HR software has worked for the past decade. You log in, you find the right module, you navigate to the right screen, you fill in the right fields. The interface was the product. Mastering the clicks was the skill — even if WalkMe helped you navigate.

That model is changing in front of us. People increasingly want to state a goal, not learn a menu. Instead of navigating three dashboards to find flight risks, a manager asks: "Show me the high-potential employees at risk of leaving my team." Natural language in, business outcome out.

This is exactly what Joule now brings to SAP SuccessFactors. The intent-based, conversational layer isn't a concept on a roadmap anymore — it's how the work gets done. And that reframes everything underneath it. The screens become less important. The system of record beneath them becomes more important, because it's what makes the intent trustworthy.

Why SuccessFactors doesn't just survive — it compounds

Here's the part the "death by AI" narrative misses. An agent is only as good as what it can reason over and act on. Strip away the interface, and the value doesn't disappear — it concentrates in the layer that most people never see.

Data depth. Agents need context to be useful, and useful HR context isn't a snapshot — it's years of employee history, compensation and performance data, and organization-specific structures. SAP SuccessFactors owns that longitudinal record, now grounded and made machine-readable through the SAP Knowledge Graph. An agent without access to this data is confidently wrong. An agent with it is genuinely helpful.

Process authority. SAP SuccessFactors doesn't just recommend — it approves, audits, documents, and proves compliance. A general-purpose AI tool can draft a suggestion. It can't stand behind a co-determination decision in a German works council, and it can't produce the audit trail your regulators expect. That authority is not a feature. It's the foundation of trust.

Platform gravity. The real advantage isn't any single agent — it's the ground they're built on. With SAP Business Technology Platform and a low-code environment for building and governing agents, the platform becomes the place where AI experiences are created, not just consumed. And because the approach is model-agnostic, you're not locked into one vendor's reasoning engine. You can bring the model you prefer, while the governance, the data, and the process control stay exactly where they belong.

That last point matters more than it first appears. SAP isn't asking you to bet on one frontier model winning the race. The moat was never the model. It's the governed data and the process authority around it.

The winning condition: governed autonomy

The Autonomous HCM vision doesn't mean full autonomy. It means governed autonomy — enough freedom for agents to drive real efficiency, and enough control to keep trust, compliance, and accountability intact.

The clearest way to make that concrete is to be explicit about boundaries. In an HR context, agents may assess risk, justify a recommendation, prepare options, and simulate outcomes. Agents must not finalize compensation, terminate employees, bypass works councils or co-determination, or make legal HR decisions on their own.

Those aren't limitations that hold the technology back. They're the guardrails that make it deployable in the real world — especially in Europe, where the compliance reality your teams live with every day is a genuine competitive advantage, not an obstacle. Generic AI can't honor those rules. A governed HR system can.

The next frontier: governing the agent workforce

Here's where this gets genuinely interesting for those of us in HR. The conversation so far has been about agents doing HR work. The next chapter is HR governing an agent workforce.

As agents scale across your organization, they start to look a lot like a new kind of workforce — one that needs to be mapped into your HR operating model, tied to org units and business functions, and understood in terms of which human skills are affected as the agents take on more. And this reshapes the operating model itself. You don't just need HR business process owners anymore. You need roles that own both the process and the agents that run it — accountable for how the two work together.

Who owns which agent? Which team relies on which capability? How does the workforce shape change as this rolls out? Answering those questions is squarely an HR job. It's the natural extension of everything HR already does well.

So what should you do about it?

If the agent era rewards depth of data, process authority, and platform gravity, then the work to get ready is refreshingly concrete. None of it is exotic. Most of it is discipline.

  1. Move to the cloud — if you haven't already. The agent capabilities described here run in the cloud, and cloud makes everything that follows much easier and faster. If parts of your core HR still runs on-premise, this is the moment to commit to the move. Everything else builds on it.
  2. Fix your data model and your data. Agents amplify whatever they're given. Feed them messy, inconsistent, incomplete data and they'll amplify the mess — faster and more confidently than any human ever could. But clean data starts with a sound model beneath it: clear organizational structures, consistent position management, and a coherent metadata model for how HR data is defined. Get the model right, then get the data clean. That combination is the fuel agents run on.
  3. Build a skills and job architecture foundation. Talent and workforce agents reason over skills. If your skills taxonomy and job architecture are vague or inconsistent, the insight the agents produce will be too.
  4. Standardize and document your processes. Process authority only helps if the processes are actually defined. Agents act on well-designed workflows, not tribal knowledge. Now is the time to capture and standardize how HR work really flows.
  5. Define your autonomy boundaries early. Don't wait for a governance question to become a governance incident. Decide up front what your agents may do, what they must not do, and where a human stays in the loop. Write it down. Involve your works councils.
  6. Invest in AI literacy and change management. The shift from clicks to intent is a shift in how people work. Your teams need to learn to lead agents, not just use software. That's a people challenge before it's a technology one.

The organizations that treat this readiness work as foundational — rather than something to bolt on later — will be the ones that turn the agent era into an advantage instead of anxiety.

What's your take?

Where do you land on this. Are you seeing "death by AI" pressure in your own HR landscape? And here's the question I'd most like your take on: where would you draw the line on what an HR agent is allowed to do?

entry.title: Re: Designing Experiential Learning in SAP S/4HANA Classrooms
entry.content:
Thank you for sharing this. I particularly liked the emphasis on connecting business context, system interaction, data and decision-making. Helping students understand why processes exist, not just how to execute them, is what makes SAP education so valuable.

The reflections on experiential learning, AI integration and teaching diverse student cohorts were also thought-provoking. Thanks for contributing to the discussion and sharing your teaching approach with the community.

entry.title: How to Transform Business Rules into Conversational Experiences Using SAP BPA and SAP Joule Studio
entry.content:
Introduction

"Am I eligible?"

"Can I proceed with this request?"

"Do I need approvals?"

"What should I do next?"

Organizations already have the answers tothese questions. They exist in policies, SOPs, decision matrices, knowledge repositories, and business processes.

The challenge is not defining business rules it is making them accessible, consistent, and available when users need guidance.

With SAP Joule Studio, organizations can deliver a conversational experience through natural language interactions. However, this raises an important architectural question:

Should AI make business decisions, or should AI provide access to decisions that are already governed by enterprise business rules?

This approach keeps decision logic governed and auditable while allowing users to interact through natural language.

By combining SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule Studio, business decisions remain governed and auditable, while users receive conversational guidance based on structured and approved business rules.

This architecture pattern is reusable across any rule-driven scenario, including eligibility assessments, approvals, compliance checks, policy guidance, benefits administration, procurement workflows, and employee self-service processes.

Business Rules Decide. SAP Joule Makes Them Conversational.

Why Keep Business Rules Outside Prompts?

When building AI assistants, it can be tempting to embed business logic directly into prompts. While this may work for simple use cases, it quickly becomes difficult to govern, audit, and maintain as complexity grows.

 Component Responsibilities

Component

Responsibility

SAP Joule Studio

Conversational experience

SAP Build Process Automation

Business decisioning and rule execution

Joule Responses

User-friendly guidance

 This blog demonstrates a practical architecture pattern for transforming governed business rules into conversational experiences using SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule Studio.

Business Scenario

Consider an employee asking: "Am I eligible for an international assignment?" SAP Build Process Automation evaluates the eligibility rules and returns a structured decision. SAP Joule Studio then converts the outcome into clear, conversational guidance.

Business Impact

  • Consistent decisioning
  • Better governance
  • Faster user support
  • Reusable services
  • Simpler AI interactions

Building the Governed Decision Layer with SAP Build Process Automation

Step 1: Create SAP Build Process Automation Project

Navigate to-->SAP Build Lobby-->Create-->Process Automation-->Create a new BPA project-->Project Name-->Save

This project will contain all rule-related artifacts and act as the business decisioning layer.

Step 2: Create Decision Services

Navigate to BPA Project--> Create-->Select Decision-->Decision Name-->Description-->Create Input Parameter--> create output parameter (if single output is required)

Step 3: Create a Reusable Result Data Type

Create a custom data type to bundle multiple decision outputs into a structured response.

Select a Datatype-->Define Name-->Add parameters with required datatype-->Save-->Close

Step 4: Rule Creation

Click Rule-->Enter Rule Name-->Description-->Select Hit policy (First match) -->Category(default)-->Next Step-->Select Input -->Next-->select output-->Select output-->Review-->Finish

Step 5: Create Rule Records

Open the Rule -->Add records manually or use Import/Export for bulk maintenance-->Save

Step 6: Create BPA Process

Create a BPA Process to orchestrate one or more Decisions and expose them as a reusable service.

Goto Create-->Process-->Add Decision(s)--> Define input/Output Variable-->click each decision box-->Map input and output variable for each decision block-->Save-->Release-->Deploy

Step 7: Publish the Process

Navigate to SAP Build Lobby -->Select Project -->Latest Version -->More Actions (⋮) --> Publish to Library.

Step 8: Test and Validate the Process

Navigate to Control Tower--> Select Environment -->Open Trigger-->Add Trigger (convert the process into API trigger) -->Save -->Open Process and Workflow--> Select process-->select Start a New Instance -->Paste JSON Payload → Run-->open monitoring-->Review the Execution Status and Outputs

Building the Conversational Layer with SAP Joule Studio

Step 1: Create Joule Project

Navigate to SAP Build Lobby -->Create -->Joule Agent and Skills -->Enter Project Name and Description -->Save.

Step 2: Add BPA Dependencies

Open Joule Project -->Click Manage Project Properties --> Dependencies --> Add -->Select the Published BPA Process/Action -->Save

Why? To allow Joule to consume business decision services without duplicating business logic.

Step 3: Invoke the BPA Process

Open the Joule Skill -->Add (+) -->Run Process -->Select the Published BPA Process--> Define Input and Output Variables -->Map the Required Process Inputs -->Save.

Important: Joule should call the process, not individual decision

Step 4: Store Process Outputs

Navigate to Joule Skill -->Add (+) -->Set Variable --> Create Custom Variables -->Select Dynamic Value -->Map Process Output Fields -->Save

Note: When using bundled custom data types, direct dynamic binding may not always be available. Using Set Variable simplifies access to individual output fields.

Step 5: Add Conditional Responses

Open the Joule Skill --> Add (+) -->Condition Branch --> Configure Conditions using the Condition Editor --> Add Response Messages for Each Outcome -->Save -->Release--> Deploy.

Step 6: Test the Conversational Experience

Open the Joule Test Panel --> Enter a sample user query --> Execute the Skill -->Verify process invocation, response generation, and expected business outcomes.

Key Challenges

Pitfall

How to address

Users enter information differently

Normalize inputs before evaluation

Rules become difficult to manage

Split logic into modular decisions

Some requests don't match any rule

Define default outcomes

AI may return inconsistent answers

Use BPA as the source of truth

Technical outputs are hard to understand

Convert outcomes into business guidance

Rules change frequently

Keep decisioning separate from conversation

 Conclusion

The goal is not to let AI make business decisions, but to make governed decisions easier to access. By combining SAP Build Process Automation with SAP Joule Studio, organizations can transform complex rules into intuitive conversational experiences.

SAP Build Process Automation decides. Joule makes it conversational.

entry.title: Bulk Upload of quality Inspection Results in SAP
entry.content:
We need a way to upload inspection results for serialized materials in bulk in SAP.
The standard "Record Inspection Results in Table form" app allows users to record inspection results, but it does not support uploading results for multiple inspection lots from an Excel file. Therefore, users must enter the inspection results manually for each inspection lot.
Each inspection lot contains 20 to 50 serial numbers. Even when the inspection result is the same for all serial numbers, users must enter the same result separately for each serial number. This makes the process slow and time-consuming.
Is there a standard SAP solution to upload inspection results in bulk from an Excel file? If not, is custom development required? If custom development is needed, please provide the recommended approach and the step-by-step process.

entry.title: Re: Set BUT000-XBLCK = X; lfa1 - sperr = 'X' and lfb1-zah...
entry.content:
I guess the BAdI CVI_CUSTOM_MAPPER may be used ONLY when maintaining a Business Partner to adjust vendor and customer specific data.

When you're maintaining the BP, maybe you could use another BAdI.

See if you can use the method IF_EX_PARTNER_UPDATE~CHANGE_BEFORE_UPDATE of the BAdI PARTNER_UPDATE, read the memory buffer to get BUT000 via the function module BUP_MEMORY_BUT000_GET, set XBLCK = 'X', write the updated data back into the BDT buffer using BUP_MEMORY_BUT000_FILL.

This proposal of changing before the update WAS NOT TESTED.

GenAI was used to help generate this content..

entry.title: Re: Subordinates transfer together with manager
entry.content: @VM90 Why would subordinates move along with the manager, when the manager changes the position? If the subordinates should move together with the manager, you might move the managers' position. But as soon as the position hierarchy is the leading one, the direct reports are still on the positions which are assigned to the managers position. And this doesn't change if you transfer the manager to another position.

entry.title: How can users trace the Address Number displayed in the Purchase Requisition?
entry.content:
How can users trace the Address Number displayed in the Purchase Requisition back to the originating Supplier/Customer Business Partner record?  

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How can users trace the Address Number displayed in t...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

Thank you for your follow-up.

Regarding the Address Number displayed in the Purchase Requisition, please note that this is an internal address identifier and it is not always directly linked to a Supplier/Customer Business Partner record. Therefore, it cannot be traced directly from the Manage Business Partner Master Data app.

If the delivery address was created using Address Type "User Entry" in the Purchase Requisition, the system creates a new address record with a unique Address Number. These addresses can later appear under Address Type "Reference" and may not exist as an address maintained in the Business Partner master data.

For more details, please refer to the SAP Knowledge Base Article:

KB0991267 – Details regarding 'Address Reference' in PO or Address type 'reference' in PR (SAP Note 0003605938)

As a recommendation, users should select existing addresses using Address Type "Reference" whenever possible to avoid duplicate address entries.

Best regards,
SAP Support Team

entry.title: Error Occured When Deleting a User in the Manage User Assignments App
entry.content:
Dear members,

We are unable to delete a user in the Manage User Assignments app.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Open the Manage User Assignments app.
2. Manually add a user.
3. Save the user.
4. Select the added user and attempt to delete them.
5. Change the user's status to Inactive and attempt to delete them again.

Actual Result:
The user cannot be deleted, even after their status is changed to Inactive. The following error message is displayed:

"An error occurred during the deletion of the object."

Could you please confirm whether this behavior is expected? If so, please provide the relevant documentation and explain how an incorrectly added or no longer required user should be removed.

Best regards,
Koichi

entry.title: Brains Meet Boundaries: Business Rules and Agentic AI in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
For years, business rules have been the backbone of automation in SAP SuccessFactors. They ensure HR processes remain consistent, compliant, and aligned with organizational policies. Whether it's validating employee data, determining recruiting template or new hire onboarding program, leave eligibility, or routing approvals, business rules have successfully automated countless HR transactions within SuccessFactors. However, the rise of Agentic AI is changing the conversation. Instead of asking, "What happens when a condition is met?", administrators are beginning to ask, "How can an AI agent achieve an HR objective while respecting our policies and governance?". This shift doesn't replace business rules, rather it elevates their role. Today, what we see is Business rules becoming the guardrails that define organizational boundaries while on the other hand, AI agents becoming the intelligent collaborators capable of orchestrating complete business outcomes. Using an objective transition from Rule-Based Automation to Goal-Based Execution, a few use cases are described below:

Business Rule: If

  • {Performance Rating ≥ 4} and {Years of Service ≥ 2}
    Then
  • {Employee is eligible for promotion}

The rule executes exactly as designed. It neither questions nor expands the decision.

AI Agent: Approaches the same scenario differently:

    • Review historical performance trends.
    • Analyze the employee's skills and certifications.
    • Evaluate succession plans.
    • Compare internal pay equity.
    • Check departmental budgets.
    • Prepare the promotion justification.
    • Route approvals.
    • Recommend leadership learning after promotion.

Output: The original business rule still determines eligibility. The AI agent simply manages everything around it to deliver the desired business outcome. This represents one of the most significant shifts in enterprise HR automation.

 

Business Rules Become Governance, Not Workflow - As organizations adopt Agentic AI, business rules evolve from being workflow drivers to becoming governance mechanisms. Instead of controlling every step of a process, they define what an AI agent is allowed to do.

Use Case 1: Hiring Process.

Traditional SuccessFactors Process

  • Validate mandatory employee information.
  • Check duplicate employee records.
  • Assign onboarding tasks.
  • Route approvals.

Agentic AI-Enabled Process
The hiring manager simply says: "Hire Priya as a Senior Consultant starting August 1."
The AI agent:

    • Collects missing information from the hiring manager.
    • Validates mandatory business rules.
    • Recommends the appropriate job classification.
    • Creates onboarding activities.
    • Coordinates IT equipment requests.
    • Books induction sessions.
    • Notifies payroll and facilities.
    • Monitors completion of onboarding tasks.

Output: You will observe that throughout the process, the AI agent never bypasses business rules. Instead, it orchestrates multiple systems while remaining compliant with organizational policies.

 

Use Case 2: Leave Management

Traditional Business Rule: {If leave balance is sufficient, approve the request.}

Agentic AI: {The AI agent evaluates}:

  • Team availability.
  • Project deadlines.
  • Public holidays.
  • Historical leave patterns.
  • Manager preferences.

Output: If the requested dates conflict with a critical project, the AI agent might recommend alternative dates that satisfy both employee preferences and business needs, while explaining the reasoning to the employee and manager.

 

Use Case 3: Internal Mobility

While business rules validate employee eligibility before applying for an internal role in recruiting, Agentic AI extends this capability for recruiters: Instead of waiting for employees to search for opportunities, an AI agent can proactively identify individuals whose skills align with upcoming vacancies within the system.

Example: An employee completes multiple cloud certifications.

The AI agent:

  • Detects the new qualifications.
  • Identifies suitable open positions.
  • Recommends additional learning to close remaining skill gaps.
  • Notifies the employee about relevant opportunities.
  • Informs HR of potential internal candidates.

Output: The business rules still determine eligibility, but the AI agent creates a far more proactive talent experience

 

Use Case 4: Cross-Module HR Orchestration

One limitation of traditional business rules is that they often operate within a single application or module, using an employee relocation to illustrate this difference.

Traditional Automation: When an employee transfers from London to Singapore:

  • Update legal entity.
  • Change payroll information.
  • Modify benefits.
  • Update organizational assignment.

Agentic AI: The AI agent can also:

  • Creates a relocation checklist.
  • Schedules tax compliance activities.
  • Recommends local cultural training.
  • Notifies facilities.
  • Coordinates IT provisioning.
  • Updates travel profiles.
  • Informs the manager of pending actions.
  • Tracks completion across all systems.

Output: Instead of automating isolated transactions, the AI agent orchestrates the entire business process.

 

Use Case 5: Intelligent Exception Handling

Traditionally, business rules are designed for known scenarios, and they often struggle with exceptions. Consider a rule that rejects candidates under the legal hiring age.

Agentic AI: An AI agent can recognize contextual nuances such as internship programs, country-specific labor regulations, or parental consent requirements. Rather than rejecting the transaction outright, it can request additional documentation or escalate the case to HR for further review.

Output: AI enables recruiters and organisations to handle exceptions more effectively without compromising compliance.

 

Business Values in Effective Governance and System Administration

As AI agents become increasingly autonomous, governance becomes a critical success factor. Organizations should establish clear policies regarding:

  • Which decisions AI agents can make independently.
  • Which decisions require human approval.
  • How AI recommendations are audited.
  • How explanations are presented to users.
  • How role-based security and data privacy are enforced.

The model described above shows that business rules remain the authoritative source of organizational policy, while AI agents operate within those predefined boundaries. The future of SAP SuccessFactors is not about replacing business rules with AI. It is about combining deterministic automation with intelligent orchestration.

Business rules continue to answer: "Is this action allowed?"

Agentic AI answers: "How can I achieve the desired outcome while complying with those rules?"

Organizations that successfully combine these two capabilities will move beyond automating individual HR tasks to orchestrating complete employee journeys from hiring and onboarding to development, mobility, compensation, and retirement. That is where the real value of Agentic AI lies: not in replacing established business processes, but in making them more intelligent, adaptive, and outcome driven.
